title: Lyme Disease
oneLiner: Tick-borne spirochaetal infection (Borrelia burgdorferi) causing rash, flu-like illness, and late neurological/cardiac/joint complications; treat early to prevent sequelae.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Bacterial meningitis (non-Lyme):** Neck stiffness, photophobia, petechial/purpuric rash, high fever – if present, start empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone IV) immediately, do not wait for Lyme serology.
- **Lyme carditis with complete heart block:** Syncope, pre-syncope, bradycardia, hypotension – ECG shows high-grade AV block, may require temporary pacing. This is a medical emergency.
- **Anaphylaxis to tick bite or antibiotic?** Uncommon but possible – look for stridor, wheeze, urticaria, hypotension.
sections.Presentation:
- **Early localised (3-30 days post bite):** Erythema migrans (EM) – expanding erythematous annular rash (≥5 cm, often with central clearing), ± flu-like symptoms (fever, malaise, myalgia, headache), but no respiratory/coryzal features.
- **Early disseminated (weeks-months):** Multiple secondary EM lesions; cranial nerve palsies (especially facial nerve/bell’s palsy); meningitis (headache, CSF lymphocytosis); carditis (palpitations, syncope, AV block); peripheral neuropathy.
- **Late (months-years):** Lyme arthritis (large joint mono/polyarthritis, intermittent); neuroborreliosis (subacute encephalopathy, radiculopathy, cognitive decline); acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans (blue-red skin changes on extensor surfaces).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral exanthem (e.g., parvovirus, EBV):** Rash not typically expanding, no tick exposure, positive serology for virus.
- **Bacterial cellulitis:** Warm, tender, non-expanding, no central clearing, systemic inflammatory response.
- **Other tick-borne infections (e.g., anaplasmosis, babesiosis):** Co-infection possible – consider if persistent fever/cytopenias despite EM treatment.
- **Idiopathic facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy):** No EM, no tick exposure, typically unilateral, but Lyme is the most common cause of bilateral facial palsy.
- **Reactive arthritis / septic arthritis (late stage):** Lyme arthritis is afebrile, non-erythematous, intermittent; septic arthritis has fever, intense pain, restricted movement.
- **Splitter:** **Bilateral facial nerve palsy** + EM rash → Lyme disease (not idiopathic Bell’s palsy).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG (look for prolonged PR interval, AV block); consider LPs if meningitis suspected (CSF: lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis, elevated protein).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP (mildly elevated or normal); Lyme serology (ELISA ± Western blot) – **do not rely on negative serology in first 4 weeks**; consider PCR on blood in early disease if rash atypical.
- **Imaging:** Not routine – echocardiogram if carditis suspected; MRI brain if neurological signs.
- **Special tests:** CSF Lyme PCR/antibody index if neuroborreliosis suspected; synovial fluid PCR for arthritis.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE guideline (NG95) – clinical diagnosis of erythema migrans is sufficient for treatment; serology confirmation recommended for disseminated/late. No formal scoring system.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any patient with syncope, palpitations, or bradycardia – Lyme carditis can present hours after EM. Also, **CSF examination** if meningitis signs present (to rule out bacterial meningitis).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If carditis with haemodynamic instability or high-grade AV block: admit to CCU, discuss cardiology for temporary pacing.
  - If suspected bacterial meningitis: IV ceftriaxone (empiric) + dexamethasone; escalate to senior immediately.
  - For stable EM: prescribe oral antibiotics (see Drugs below) and discharge with safety netting.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For meningitis/neuroborreliosis: IV ceftriaxone 21 days (or oral doxycycline 21 days if mild).
  - For carditis: IV ceftriaxone ± telemetry; monitor ECG daily; pacemaker if block persists.
  - For arthritis: oral doxycycline 28 days; if refractory, consider IV ceftriaxone.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete prescribed course (do not stop early; do not prolong beyond NICE duration).
  - Refer to infectious diseases clinic if complicated (neuro, carditis, arthritis, treatment failure).
  - Advise tick avoidance measures.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h maintenance (if nil by mouth).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk).
  - Diet: normal (unless meningitis necessitating NBM for LP).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; if carditis, continuous cardiac monitoring.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Early localised (erythema migrans):** Doxycycline 100 mg oral twice daily for 21 days. (NICE NG95)
  - **Alternative (contraindicated to doxycycline):** Amoxicillin 500 mg oral three times daily for 21 days.
  - **Disseminated/neurological/carditis (non-life-threatening):** Doxycycline 100 mg oral/IV twice daily for 21 days.
  - **Severe neurological/meningitis/carditis (IV needed):** Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily for 21 days.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: caution in children <12 years and pregnancy (avoid); avoid antacids, iron, calcium rich food within 2 hours.
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates (bilirubin displacement); caution in renal impairment (adjust).
  - Amoxicillin: avoid if penicillin allergy (consider cefuroxime axetil).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment in mild-moderate renal impairment; reduce dose if CrCl <10 mL/min.
  - Amoxicillin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 250 mg three times daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Missed doxycycline interaction with antacids/iron** — reduces absorption, leading to treatment failure. Counsel patient to take doxycycline on empty stomach and separate from antacids by 2 hours.
  - **Stopping antibiotics early** — increases risk of disseminated disease. Always complete 21 days.
  - **Overuse of IV ceftriaxone for simple EM** — IV not needed; can cause line infections and C. diff.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Complete heart block from Lyme carditis (can be fatal if untreated)
  - Meningitis/encephalitis (rarely raised ICP)
  - Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension 2-24 hours after first antibiotic dose – treat supportively, do not stop antibiotics)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-Lyme syndrome (persistent fatigue, arthralgia, cognitive symptoms – not responsive to prolonged antibiotics; refer to specialist)
  - Chronic Lyme arthritis (requires repeat course or SAARDs)
  - Irreversible facial nerve palsy (if delayed treatment)
  - Late neuroborreliosis (peripheral neuropathy, encephalomyelitis)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent if treated early – >90% cure with doxycycline. Untreated, up to 60% develop arthritis and 15% neurological complications. Mortality extremely rare (<0.5%) but carditis can be fatal. Post-Lyme syndrome occurs in ~10% but resolves over months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **If you see an expanding target-like rash >5 cm in a patient with tick exposure, start doxycycline immediately – do not wait for serology.**
- **In any acute facial nerve palsy (especially bilateral), take a tick history and examine for EM – treat empirically if high suspicion.**
- **Always check ECG before calling reg for syncope in a young patient – if AV block present, think Lyme.**
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on negative ELISA in early disease** – seroconversion takes 4-6 weeks. Treat based on clinical rash.
- **Do not prescribe steroids for Bell’s palsy until Lyme serology is negative** – steroids can worsen disseminated Lyme.
- **Do not give prophylactic antibiotics after tick bite** – not recommended in UK (risk outweighs benefit). Advise watchful waiting and tick removal.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New rash, severe headache, facial droop, palpitations, syncope, joint swelling, or fever >38°C not resolving after 7 days.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2 weeks to check treatment completion and symptom resolution. If persistent or neurological/cardiac symptoms, refer to infectious diseases.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2mmol/L; new bradycardia <40 or syncope (possible carditis); suspected meningitis (GCS<15, neck stiffness, focal neurology); deterioration despite antibiotics.
- **Call ICU if:** Haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors; high-grade AV block with compromise; GCS <8 or seizures.

rawMarkdown:
# Lyme Disease
**One-liner:** Tick-borne spirochaetal infection (Borrelia burgdorferi) causing rash, flu-like illness, and late neurological/cardiac/joint complications; treat early to prevent sequelae.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Bacterial meningitis (non-Lyme):** Neck stiffness, photophobia, petechial/purpuric rash, high fever – if present, start empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone IV) immediately, do not wait for Lyme serology.
- **Lyme carditis with complete heart block:** Syncope, pre-syncope, bradycardia, hypotension – ECG shows high-grade AV block, may require temporary pacing. This is a medical emergency.
- **Anaphylaxis to tick bite or antibiotic?** Uncommon but possible – look for stridor, wheeze, urticaria, hypotension.

### Presentation
- **Early localised (3-30 days post bite):** Erythema migrans (EM) – expanding erythematous annular rash (≥5 cm, often with central clearing), ± flu-like symptoms (fever, malaise, myalgia, headache), but no respiratory/coryzal features.
- **Early disseminated (weeks-months):** Multiple secondary EM lesions; cranial nerve palsies (especially facial nerve/bell’s palsy); meningitis (headache, CSF lymphocytosis); carditis (palpitations, syncope, AV block); peripheral neuropathy.
- **Late (months-years):** Lyme arthritis (large joint mono/polyarthritis, intermittent); neuroborreliosis (subacute encephalopathy, radiculopathy, cognitive decline); acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans (blue-red skin changes on extensor surfaces).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral exanthem (e.g., parvovirus, EBV):** Rash not typically expanding, no tick exposure, positive serology for virus.
- **Bacterial cellulitis:** Warm, tender, non-expanding, no central clearing, systemic inflammatory response.
- **Other tick-borne infections (e.g., anaplasmosis, babesiosis):** Co-infection possible – consider if persistent fever/cytopenias despite EM treatment.
- **Idiopathic facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy):** No EM, no tick exposure, typically unilateral, but Lyme is the most common cause of bilateral facial palsy.
- **Reactive arthritis / septic arthritis (late stage):** Lyme arthritis is afebrile, non-erythematous, intermittent; septic arthritis has fever, intense pain, restricted movement.
- **Splitter:** **Bilateral facial nerve palsy** + EM rash → Lyme disease (not idiopathic Bell’s palsy).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG (look for prolonged PR interval, AV block); consider LPs if meningitis suspected (CSF: lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis, elevated protein).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP (mildly elevated or normal); Lyme serology (ELISA ± Western blot) – **do not rely on negative serology in first 4 weeks**; consider PCR on blood in early disease if rash atypical.
- **Imaging:** Not routine – echocardiogram if carditis suspected; MRI brain if neurological signs.
- **Special tests:** CSF Lyme PCR/antibody index if neuroborreliosis suspected; synovial fluid PCR for arthritis.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE guideline (NG95) – clinical diagnosis of erythema migrans is sufficient for treatment; serology confirmation recommended for disseminated/late. No formal scoring system.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any patient with syncope, palpitations, or bradycardia – Lyme carditis can present hours after EM. Also, **CSF examination** if meningitis signs present (to rule out bacterial meningitis).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If carditis with haemodynamic instability or high-grade AV block: admit to CCU, discuss cardiology for temporary pacing.
  - If suspected bacterial meningitis: IV ceftriaxone (empiric) + dexamethasone; escalate to senior immediately.
  - For stable EM: prescribe oral antibiotics (see Drugs below) and discharge with safety netting.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For meningitis/neuroborreliosis: IV ceftriaxone 21 days (or oral doxycycline 21 days if mild).
  - For carditis: IV ceftriaxone ± telemetry; monitor ECG daily; pacemaker if block persists.
  - For arthritis: oral doxycycline 28 days; if refractory, consider IV ceftriaxone.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete prescribed course (do not stop early; do not prolong beyond NICE duration).
  - Refer to infectious diseases clinic if complicated (neuro, carditis, arthritis, treatment failure).
  - Advise tick avoidance measures.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h maintenance (if nil by mouth).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk).
  - Diet: normal (unless meningitis necessitating NBM for LP).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; if carditis, continuous cardiac monitoring.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Early localised (erythema migrans):** Doxycycline 100 mg oral twice daily for 21 days. (NICE NG95)
  - **Alternative (contraindicated to doxycycline):** Amoxicillin 500 mg oral three times daily for 21 days.
  - **Disseminated/neurological/carditis (non-life-threatening):** Doxycycline 100 mg oral/IV twice daily for 21 days.
  - **Severe neurological/meningitis/carditis (IV needed):** Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily for 21 days.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: caution in children <12 years and pregnancy (avoid); avoid antacids, iron, calcium rich food within 2 hours.
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates (bilirubin displacement); caution in renal impairment (adjust).
  - Amoxicillin: avoid if penicillin allergy (consider cefuroxime axetil).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment in mild-moderate renal impairment; reduce dose if CrCl <10 mL/min.
  - Amoxicillin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 250 mg three times daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Missed doxycycline interaction with antacids/iron** — reduces absorption, leading to treatment failure. Counsel patient to take doxycycline on empty stomach and separate from antacids by 2 hours.
  - **Stopping antibiotics early** — increases risk of disseminated disease. Always complete 21 days.
  - **Overuse of IV ceftriaxone for simple EM** — IV not needed; can cause line infections and C. diff.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Complete heart block from Lyme carditis (can be fatal if untreated)
  - Meningitis/encephalitis (rarely raised ICP)
  - Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension 2-24 hours after first antibiotic dose – treat supportively, do not stop antibiotics)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-Lyme syndrome (persistent fatigue, arthralgia, cognitive symptoms – not responsive to prolonged antibiotics; refer to specialist)
  - Chronic Lyme arthritis (requires repeat course or SAARDs)
  - Irreversible facial nerve palsy (if delayed treatment)
  - Late neuroborreliosis (peripheral neuropathy, encephalomyelitis)

### Prognosis
- Excellent if treated early – >90% cure with doxycycline. Untreated, up to 60% develop arthritis and 15% neurological complications. Mortality extremely rare (<0.5%) but carditis can be fatal. Post-Lyme syndrome occurs in ~10% but resolves over months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **If you see an expanding target-like rash >5 cm in a patient with tick exposure, start doxycycline immediately – do not wait for serology.**
- **In any acute facial nerve palsy (especially bilateral), take a tick history and examine for EM – treat empirically if high suspicion.**
- **Always check ECG before calling reg for syncope in a young patient – if AV block present, think Lyme.**

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on negative ELISA in early disease** – seroconversion takes 4-6 weeks. Treat based on clinical rash.
- **Do not prescribe steroids for Bell’s palsy until Lyme serology is negative** – steroids can worsen disseminated Lyme.
- **Do not give prophylactic antibiotics after tick bite** – not recommended in UK (risk outweighs benefit). Advise watchful waiting and tick removal.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New rash, severe headache, facial droop, palpitations, syncope, joint swelling, or fever >38°C not resolving after 7 days.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2 weeks to check treatment completion and symptom resolution. If persistent or neurological/cardiac symptoms, refer to infectious diseases.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2mmol/L; new bradycardia <40 or syncope (possible carditis); suspected meningitis (GCS<15, neck stiffness, focal neurology); deterioration despite antibiotics.
- **Call ICU if:** Haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors; high-grade AV block with compromise; GCS <8 or seizures.
